head_coach.name: Nick Sirianni
head_coach.scheme_philosophy: Offensive background with CEO-style management; historically collaborative with OCs on playcalling; emphasizes run-game dominance and physicality; won Super Bowl LIX after 2024 season

offensive_coordinator.name: Sean Mannion
offensive_coordinator.notes: First-time playcaller; spent 9 years as NFL backup QB, then 2 years coaching in Green Bay under Matt LaFleur (offensive assistant 2024, QBs coach 2025); played under Sean McVay with Rams and Kevin O'Connell with Vikings; hired Feb 2026 to replace fired Kevin Patullo; bringing Shanahan-McVay system to Philadelphia with emphasis on wide zone, play action, pre-snap motion, and under-center usage

defensive_coordinator.name: Vic Fangio
defensive_coordinator.scheme_tree: Fangio 3-4 / split-field zone coverage system (original innovator)
defensive_coordinator.notes: 67 years old, returning for 3rd season as Eagles DC after contemplating retirement; 41-year coaching veteran; created the modern two-high disguised coverage system now widely copied across NFL; full autonomy over defensive playcalling

offense.scheme_family: Shanahan/McVay wide zone (transitioning in 2026 from West Coast/Sirianni hybrid)
offense.base_personnel: 11 and 12 (expect significant 12 personnel increase under Mannion; LaFleur's Packers used 12 personnel on 33-37% of snaps)
offense.run_scheme: wide zone primary, with gap/power complementary; boot and play-action concepts married to outside zone; expect more under-center looks than 2025 (Eagles were 27th in under-center usage in 2025)
offense.pass_concepts: play action heavy off wide zone, boot concepts with three-level flood routes, pre-snap motion to create leverage and disguise intent; timing-based passing with QB-friendly progressions; emphasis on tying run and pass together
offense.rpo_usage: moderate to low (Shanahan-McVay tree relies more on traditional play action than RPOs, though Hurts' athleticism will preserve some RPO elements)
offense.te_usage: critical to scheme; Shanahan/LaFleur systems feature TEs as both inline blockers and receiving weapons; expect move TE/flex usage in 12 personnel; TE leak routes off split zone and boot concepts are staples; Eagles losing Dallas Goedert in free agency creates major need at position; hired Ryan Mahaffey as TE coach/run game coordinator
offense.rb_usage: bell cow runner in Saquon Barkley; Shanahan-style values dual-threat backs who can run choice routes; Barkley has elite receiving traits but was underutilized in passing game in 2025 (47.2 PFF receiving grade); expect increased receiving role under Mannion's system

defense.alignment: odd front (3 down linemen with 2 stand-up OLBs in base); shifts to 4-2 nickel front against 11 personnel with one LB removed; versatile fronts that toggle between 3-4 base and 4-2 nickel
defense.edge_rush_source: hybrid; stand-up OLBs in base 3-4 (Jaelan Phillips, Nolan Smith, Jalyx Hunt) who rush as primary edge players; in nickel 4-2, edges can be hand-in-dirt or two-point stance; Fangio values versatile edge players who can stand up or put hand down
defense.coverage_base: split-field zone with two-high shell disguise; primarily quarters (Cover 4), Cover 6 (quarter-quarter-half), and Cover 2/Cover 3 rotations post-snap; heavy coverage disguise pre-snap with interchangeable safeties; targeted coverages that roll to opponent's best receiver
defense.blitz_tendency: light (historically bottom-5 in NFL; 19.3% in 2024 Super Bowl season; increased to ~22-27% in 2025 due to edge personnel turnover but trended back to low rates by late season; Fangio's philosophy is to generate pressure with four-man rush and keep seven in coverage)
defense.lb_role: ILBs (Zack Baun, Nakobe Dean, Jihaad Campbell) are coverage-first with run-stopping duties; Baun is elite in coverage; Campbell has EDGE/LB hybrid versatility; OLBs are primary pass rushers; in nickel (majority of snaps), Eagles often play with only 1 off-ball LB
defense.safety_usage: interchangeable safeties are crucial to Fangio's system; both safeties start in two-high shell pre-snap to disguise coverage; strong safety used as C-gap run fitter; free safety can rotate to box or stay deep; Reed Blankenship (pending FA) and Andrew Mukuba fill hybrid roles; Cooper DeJean also plays in slot/nickel role

te_receiving_boost.explanation: The transition to Mannion's Shanahan/McVay/LaFleur system massively elevates TE value. LaFleur's Packers used 12 personnel on 33-37% of snaps — among the highest rates in the NFL — with TEs as featured receivers on boot concepts, leak routes off split zone, and three-level floods. Dallas Goedert (11 TDs in 2025) is expected to leave in free agency, creating a massive void. The Eagles hired Ryan Mahaffey specifically as TE coach/run game coordinator from the same scheme tree. A receiving TE who can also block in the run game is arguably their highest-value draft target. Sirianni stated TEs must 'create mismatches in the pass game' and be 'great yards-after-catch guys' while also being 'effective in the run game.' This system treats an elite receiving TE as a WR-level weapon.

lb_pass_rush_boost.explanation: Fangio's 3-4 base uses stand-up OLBs as primary edge rushers. Aldon Smith had 42 sacks as a stand-up edge under Fangio in SF. Khalil Mack was All-Pro in Fangio's Bears defense. The Eagles' current OLB room (Phillips, Nolan Smith, Jalyx Hunt) are all stand-up rushers technically listed as LBs. Additionally, ILB Jihaad Campbell has EDGE/LB hybrid flexibility that Fangio values — he can drop to the edge and rush. Any LB prospect with elite pass rush traits (burst, bend, power) fits this defense as a stand-up edge rusher and should be valued at EDGE prices. Fangio also values ILBs who can be effective blitzers (Nakobe Dean was a praised blitzer).

rb_dual_threat_boost.explanation: The Shanahan/McVay/LaFleur system specifically values pass-catching RBs. LaFleur has stated he 'prefers running backs who can run choice routes and catch flawlessly.' Saquon Barkley is the bell cow but his receiving usage was historically low in Philly's old scheme. Under the new system, the RB is a key outlet in play-action and boot concepts. Barkley was more active as a receiver in 2025 camp and Mannion's scheme should unlock this further. Additionally, the Eagles carry Will Shipley as a receiving back complement. For draft purposes, an RB who can execute as a receiver in this system gets a significant value boost — the Shanahan tree produced receiving backs like Christian McCaffrey and Austin Ekeler as scheme-elevated weapons.

s_hybrid_boost.explanation: Fangio's system requires interchangeable safeties as a foundational principle. Both safeties must be able to play deep, rotate into the box, cover slot receivers, and fit the run from the C-gap. Pre-snap, both safeties show the same two-high look regardless of their post-snap assignment, which demands versatility. Fangio's scheme is built on disguise — a safety who can only play one role undermines the entire coverage system. Reed Blankenship is a pending free agent, creating a need. Andrew Mukuba showed promise but had early struggles. A safety prospect with hybrid traits (box tackling + deep range + slot coverage ability) is extremely valuable to this defense because the system literally cannot function without versatile safeties.

scheme_specific_draft_notes: The Eagles enter the 2026 draft at a scheme inflection point. On offense, Sean Mannion is installing a Shanahan/McVay/LaFleur wide zone system that demands fundamentally different skill sets than the prior West Coast approach. The biggest positional need is TE — with Goedert reportedly leaving and no other viable TEs under contract, the Eagles desperately need a receiving TE who can also block in 12 personnel. Any TE prospect with route-running, YAC ability, and adequate inline blocking should be valued significantly higher for PHI. The LaFleur system makes TEs WR-level weapons on boot concepts and seam routes. On defense, Fangio's 3-4 with stand-up OLB edge rushers means any LB with pass-rush traits should be valued like an EDGE for PHI. The system also demands interchangeable safeties — a traditional deep-only free safety has less value here than a versatile S who can play box, slot, and deep. Blitz rate is historically low, so the Eagles need edge rushers who can win 1-on-1 without help, not blitz-scheme dependent players. ILBs must be coverage-first, as Fangio often plays only 1 off-ball LB in nickel. RBs with receiving ability get a boost in Mannion's scheme due to the play-action/boot passing game that features RB outlets. With A.J. Brown potentially traded and the offense in transition, pass-catching skill players at every position get elevated consideration.
